content.text: The extends keyword lets one interface inherit all members of another. The child interface gains every property and method of its parent, and can add new ones or narrow existing types.

attrs.alt: Interface inheritance hierarchy: Entity → User → Admin / Moderator
attrs.title: Interface Hierarchy

content.text: A single interface can extend more than one parent, combining all their members. This is TypeScript's way of expressing interface-level multiple inheritance, which is safe because interfaces are type-only constructs — there is no runtime diamond-problem.

Interface extension (extends) and declaration merging both add members to an interface, but they work differently. Extends creates a named child that inherits; merging augments the same interface across multiple declarations.
A child interface can narrow (restrict) an inherited property type to a subtype. The narrowed type must be assignable to the parent type — widening is not allowed.

Extending library interfaces avoids modifying their source while adding domain-specific members. This is the preferred technique for adding typed properties to DOM types, Express requests, or Angular router data.
Interface Extension Chain — Depth and Pitfalls

Interfaces can form chains of arbitrary depth. Each level inherits all members from every ancestor. Deep chains can make it harder to find where a property originates; use them for genuine hierarchy, not grouping.
Generic interfaces can extend other generic or non-generic interfaces. Type parameters flow through the hierarchy just like concrete types.
Extending vs Intersection — When to Choose
Interface extension and type intersection (&) both combine shapes, but they differ in syntax, error messages, and whether declaration merging applies.
Can be verbose for deep intersections
Results in never for incompatible props
Yes — only possible with type alias

TypeScript does not support multiple class inheritance but interfaces can extend multiple sources. The mixin pattern combines this with object spread to compose behavior from many independent features.
Interface Extension in Angular Dependency Injection
Angular's DI system uses interfaces as tokens for service contracts. Extending interfaces ensures that implementations include all required members while allowing specialisation.
Narrowing via Extension — Only Allowed for Compatible Types
Declaration Merging vs Extension — Scoping Rules
Merged — single property in the result
Use one base with index sig

Interface extension (extends) lets one interface inherit all members of one or more parents, forming typed hierarchies. This is the right tool when shapes have genuine IS-A relationships, library types need augmentation, or a class needs a multi-level contract.
— B carries all A's members plus its own.
— safe because interfaces have no runtime implementation.
Inherited properties can be narrowed (subtype) but not widened or type-changed.
Declaration merging augments an existing interface; extends creates a new named type — both are useful, neither replaces the other.
Extend built-in and third-party interfaces (DOM, Express, Angular) in
Generic interfaces propagate type parameters through the extension chain.
Prefer extends for object hierarchies; prefer & for ad-hoc utility composition or when a mapped/conditional type is involved.
